EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms again developed across Utah on August 11, and a severe wind gust was recorded from one of these storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm dropped heavy rain in the area of Woodland Hills, Utah, leading to a large mudslide in a residential area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm dropped very heavy rainfall near Woodland Hills and, combined with saturated soil from large amounts of rain the previous day, produced a large mudslide across the area. The mud damaged the basement of one home and also invaded five other properties, with extensive mud clean-up necessary on private driveways and along Skylake Drive. A total of 14 homes were evacuated as a precaution immediately following the mudslide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across the southern half of Utah on August 22, and flash flooding was reported at Capitol Reef National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Park rangers at Capitol Reef National Park reported flash flooding at multiple locations in the park. Flooding first occurred along Sulphur Creek, and then a second thunderstorm initiated flash flooding along Grand Wash, with flood waters reaching Highway 24.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and windy storm system moved through Utah beginning April 14, and the winds caused widespread damage across the state. This damage included a fatal pileup accident on Interstate 80 and a destructive wildfire in central Utah that was fueled by the winds. In addition, the storm brought heavy snowfall to both the mountains and valleys of northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind guts included 71 mph at the U.S. 6 at Eureka sensor, 62 mph at the Delta Municipal Airport, 61 mph at Fish Springs in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet, and 58 mph at the Clearspot sensor. Blowing dust reduced visibilities and created hazardous driving conditions across the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall included 12 inches in Bountiful, 12 inches on the Northeast Ogden Bench, 11 inches in Ogden, and many other reports along the northern Wasatch Front of 5-10 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and strong southwest flow across the region preceded an upper trough which crossed over the area late on March 2nd through March 3rd.  This situation resulted in a prolonged and nearly continuous snowfall event mainly for the mountain areas of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This storm event which continued into the evening of March 3rd produced total snowfall amounts of 1 to 3 feet across the area.  Locally higher amounts included just over 4 feet at the Buckboard Flat SNOTEL site.  Wind gusts of 25 to 45 mph produced areas of blowing and drifting snow.  Locally stronger peak winds included a gust to 71 mph on La Sal Mountain just below the 10,000 foot level and a gust to 55 mph on Abajo Peak near the 11,000 foot level.

This thunderstorm caused significant damage in the town of Portage, Utah, with several trees damaged or knocked down and approximately ten homes with shingle damage. In addition, a porch awning collapsed, injuring a woman and sending her to the hospital.

The most dramatic flooding occurred in Crystal Springs Canyon, where visitors to the lake watched as three of their boats were tossed around and damaged by the flood waters. One of these boats, a 23-foot 1997 Maxum, was hit and submerged by two boulders, and was considered a total loss after the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper trough over the western United States initially brought snow to northeast Utah.  As the trough deepened over the west, the main area of snowfall transitioned to southeast Utah as the flow aloft became southwesterly.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured snowfall amounts of 11 to 16 inches were reported from the official cooperative observation stations.  However, heavier amounts up to 2 and 3 feet deep were reported on Navajo Nation lands in San Juan County, with drifts waist-high in some locations.  This situation made local roads impassable and caused many families to be snowed in, which at one point was estimated to be up to 350 families. The Navajo Nation���s Emergency Operations Center (EOC) was activated, and the Navajo chapters in southeast Utah declared a State of Emergency. CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and windy storm system moved through Utah beginning April 14, and the winds caused widespread damage across the state. This damage included a fatal pileup accident on Interstate 80 and a destructive wildfire in central Utah that was fueled by the winds. In addition, the storm brought heavy snowfall to both the mountains and valleys of northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The winds were strongest across Utah's northwest desert, with maximum wind gusts of 82 mph at both Lakeside Mountain and English Village, 81 mph at the Interstate 80 at Milepost 1 sensor, 75 mph at Badger Island, 74 mph at Upper Cedar Mountain, 72 mph at the Wendover Point of Entry, and many observations in the 55-70 mph range. The largest impact was along Interstate 80 near Delle, where low visibility in blowing dust was a major factor in a large pilup crash. A semitruck slowed down when experiencing reduced visibilities, but this caused several passenger vehicles to collide with the back of the truck. After the chain reaction was finished, a total of 6 semitrucks and 11 passenger vehicles were involved in the accident, resulting in 25 injuries, including 18 hospitalizations. There was also one fatality in the crash, a 65-year-old male. The interstate was closed for 6 hours from Milepost 1 to Milepost 99 due to this accident. In separate incidents, several other semitrailers were also knocked over along Interstate 80 due to the strong crosswinds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and windy storm system moved through Utah beginning April 14, and the winds caused widespread damage across the state. This damage included a fatal pileup accident on Interstate 80 and a destructive wildfire in central Utah that was fueled by the winds. In addition, the storm brought heavy snowfall to both the mountains and valleys of northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum wind gusts recorded in the Salt Lake and Tooele valleys included 81 mph at the Great Salt Lake Marina, 75 mph at Baccus Highway, 66 mph at Point of the Mountain, 66 mph at the Interstate 15 at 2400 South sensor, 65 mph at Salt Air, and 64 mph at the Salt Lake City International Airport ASOS. A semitrailer was overturned on Interstate 215 in Salt Lake City, and several large trees were uprooted across the Salt Lake Valley. Winds also caused damage to many fences and yards across the area, including displacing sheds and knocking over at least one cinder block wall.  Salt Lake City International Airport had an internal ground stop of 1 hour and 45 minutes total due to the strong winds; 25 aircraft were held for up to 63 minutes, with an average of a 30 minute delay, and at least 16 flights were diverted to other airports. Power outages were common across the area, with at least 9,000 customers impacted in Salt Lake County and over 2,000 customers impacted in Tooele County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and windy storm system moved through Utah beginning April 14, and the winds caused widespread damage across the state. This damage included a fatal pileup accident on Interstate 80 and a destructive wildfire in central Utah that was fueled by the winds. In addition, the storm brought heavy snowfall to both the mountains and valleys of northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall in the Salt Lake Valley included 14 inches at Upper Millcreek, 12 inches in Sandy, and many reports of approximately 10 inches of snow. The worst conditions on roadways occurred on the morning commute of April 15, with 118 crashes reported, including 16 that resulted in injuries. During the evening commute on April 15, a woman was killed in a multicar crash on State Route 201 near the Interstate 80 on-ramp. The victim lost control of her vehicle on the snow-packed road, and was then rear-ended by another vehicle traveling the same route. In addition, a Utah Highway Patrol trooper was injured while responding to the scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the snowfall of the first three days of March, a snowboarder was caught and killed in an avalanche in Hells Canyon, just outside Snowbasin Resort.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Following a very dry and warm winter, two friends wanted to take advantage of the fresh snowfall from early March, so they decided to go snowboarding at Snowbasin Resort on March 4. On one of their runs, they made their way out-of-bounds of the resort in an area called Hells Canyon. On their way out of this terrain, one of the snowboarders, a 21-year-old male, triggered an avalanche. He was caught and buried in the avalanche, while his trailing friend stayed out of the path and was able to find help. However, when the body of the victim was found an hour later, he was determined to have injuries incompatible with life, and he passed away that day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms were observed across primarily northern Utah from August 5  through August 7, with significant wind damage on multiple days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds destroyed a wooden barn in Bothwell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms were observed across primarily northern Utah from August 5  through August 7, with significant wind damage on multiple days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The White Reef RAWS recorded a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms were observed across primarily northern Utah from August 5  through August 7, with significant wind damage on multiple days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were knocked down by thunderstorm winds near Henefer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms continued across southern Utah during the last week of August, with a couple of flash floods reported in far southern Utah. A lightning fatality also occurred in northern Utah during this period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain caused a massive flash flood along Wahweap Creek. The flood wave was caught on video and it carried full size dead trees left over from previous smaller floods, along with boulders the size of cars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms continued across southern Utah during the last week of August, with a couple of flash floods reported in far southern Utah. A lightning fatality also occurred in northern Utah during this period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A family was having a picnic in a park in Lehi, Utah, when a thunderstorm moved across the area. Lightning struck a nearby tree, and critically injured a 50-year-old woman. No one else in the party was seriously injured, but the victim passed away in a hospital a few days later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across far northwest Utah on October 1, producing severe wind gusts. The thunderstorms that developed on October 2 had access to more moisture, producing some flash flooding along the Wasatch Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Callao sensor in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet recorded a peak wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across far northwest Utah on October 1, producing severe wind gusts. The thunderstorms that developed on October 2 had access to more moisture, producing some flash flooding along the Wasatch Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Causeway sensor in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet recorded a maximum wind gust of 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across far northwest Utah on October 1, producing severe wind gusts. The thunderstorms that developed on October 2 had access to more moisture, producing some flash flooding along the Wasatch Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 64 mph was recorded at the West Salt Flats sensor in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across far northwest Utah on October 1, producing severe wind gusts. The thunderstorms that developed on October 2 had access to more moisture, producing some flash flooding along the Wasatch Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 72 mph wind gust was recorded at the North Salt Flats sensor in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across far northwest Utah on October 1, producing severe wind gusts. The thunderstorms that developed on October 2 had access to more moisture, producing some flash flooding along the Wasatch Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain brought road, parking lot, and basement flooding to the Sugarhouse and Foothill areas of Salt Lake City. The flooding damaged multiple streets including Roxberry Road, Sherwood Drive, and 1300 South near 2500 East. There was also a significant amount of water on Foothill Drive, particularly near 2200 South. Approximately six homes also experienced basement flooding across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across far northwest Utah on October 1, producing severe wind gusts. The thunderstorms that developed on October 2 had access to more moisture, producing some flash flooding along the Wasatch Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported in Lehi, with some flooded residential roadways. In addition, approximately six homes sustained flood damage, primarily to basements.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across far northwest Utah on October 1, producing severe wind gusts. The thunderstorms that developed on October 2 had access to more moisture, producing some flash flooding along the Wasatch Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain brought road, parking lot, and basement flooding to the Sugarhouse and Foothill areas of Salt Lake City. The flooding damaged multiple streets including Roxberry Road, Sherwood Drive, and 1300 South near 2500 East. There was also a significant amount of water on Foothill Drive, particularly near 2200 South. Approximately six homes also experienced basement flooding across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep subtropical moisture moved into Utah on October 5, with showers and thunderstorms producing heavy rain across the state. Some of these storms caused flash flooding in central and southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported in several locations, including Deer Creek and Calf Creek. The Dirty Devil gauge at Poison Springs rose from 23 cfs to 1176 cfs, and part of Hole-in-the-Rock Road was washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep subtropical moisture moved into Utah on October 5, with showers and thunderstorms producing heavy rain across the state. Some of these storms caused flash flooding in central and southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported in Capitol Reef National Park, with one foot of water over the Capitol Gorge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of strong thunderstorms formed across Utah for a few days in the middle of October. Severe wind gusts were observed in northwest Utah, while some flash flooding occurred in southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding across Hildale, with multiple roads damaged by the flood waters. Debris was flowing along the shoulder of State Route 59 between Milepost 0.5 and Milepost 1.0, and minor flooding occurred along Carling Avenue and Utah Avenue. In addition, several homes reported basement flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of strong thunderstorms formed across Utah for a few days in the middle of October. Severe wind gusts were observed in northwest Utah, while some flash flooding occurred in southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wendover Airport AWOS recorded a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of strong thunderstorms formed across Utah for a few days in the middle of October. Severe wind gusts were observed in northwest Utah, while some flash flooding occurred in southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gunnison Island mesonet site recorded a maximum wind gust of 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of strong thunderstorms formed across Utah for a few days in the middle of October. Severe wind gusts were observed in northwest Utah, while some flash flooding occurred in southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported in several locations across Zion National Park. This flooding included the Emerald Pools area, but most of the flooding occurred on the east side of the park, including Spry Canyon, Pine Creek Canyon, and a few other small canyons nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of strong thunderstorms formed across Utah for a few days in the middle of October. Severe wind gusts were observed in northwest Utah, while some flash flooding occurred in southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported along Wahweap Creek, Buckskin Gulch, and the lower portions of the Paria River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of strong thunderstorms formed across Utah for a few days in the middle of October. Severe wind gusts were observed in northwest Utah, while some flash flooding occurred in southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home in Cedar City and started a fire in one corner of the house. Firefighters were able to put out the fire relatively quickly, but not before the residence sustained approximately $8,000 worth of damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The end of May produced very active weather across the state of Utah, with widespread thunderstorms on most days. Several flash floods were observed across southern Utah, and multiple severe thunderstorms occurred across the northern half of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported along Wahweap Creek and some surrounding drainages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms moved through far northern Utah on June 1, producing severe, damaging wind gusts in multiple locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rosebud RAWS recorded a peak wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prolonged rainfall along the Salt Wash basin caused minor flooding in the Arches National Park area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wolf Ranch and Delicate Arch roads in Arches National Park were closed due to at least several inches of water running over the roadways from the local washes due to a prolonged rain event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms formed across far northern Utah on September 5, with one storm causing wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few medium sized trees were knocked down by strong thunderstorm winds near Logan, with one tree falling onto a car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms formed across primarily central Utah on September 1, with heavy rain causing flash flooding in Capitol Reef National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding was reported in Capitol Reef National Park, with multiple flood waves reported along Sulphur Creek. Flash flooding also occurred in both Spring Canyon and Chimney Rock Canyon. The Spring Canyon flooding was generally between 6 inches and two feet deep, though it reached as high as 5 feet deep through the narrow section. Water flowing through Chimney Rock Canyon was reported to be one foot deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple strong thunderstorms producing heavy rainfall moved through southwest Utah on September 14, creating significant flash flooding in Hildale, Utah and Zion National Park. These two flash flood events combined to produce the deadliest natural disaster in Utah history, with 20 fatalities in the state of Utah, and a 21st just south of the border in Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm producing heavy rain moved over Hildale, Utah in the early afternoon, producing flash flooding along Short Creek and other neighboring drainages. This water flooded Highway 59, and portions of the road were closed and gated off, as this is a somewhat common occurrence for the community. However, a second strong thunderstorm trained through the area, producing very significant flooding out of Maxwell and Jans Canyons.||This second flood swept across a group of 16 people who had been waiting for the initial flood waters to recede. When the second flood began, the group retreated to the two vans they had been traveling in, and the large flood swept the vans away and down the channel. Three of these people were rescued and survived, but the remaining 13 people were killed by the flood. Three of these 13 victims were women, while the remaining 10 were children under the age of 12.||Property damage was also extensive across the area, including damage to water lines, area roadways, power lines, bridges, and other infrastructure. In addition, multiple private homes had flood and mud damage, while several others had minor water damage. Multiple vehicles were also destroyed in the flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple strong thunderstorms producing heavy rainfall moved through southwest Utah on September 14, creating significant flash flooding in Hildale, Utah and Zion National Park. These two flash flood events combined to produce the deadliest natural disaster in Utah history, with 20 fatalities in the state of Utah, and a 21st just south of the border in Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of heavy rainfall brought flash flooding to Zion National Park, including along North Creek, but the most notable flood occurred in Keyhole Canyon. A group of seven people entered Keyhole Canyon in the early afternoon, planning to travel through the small but popular canyoneering route. Flooding in Keyhole Canyon occurred quickly; no one in the group was able to escape the canyon or get to higher ground, and the flood killed all seven individuals. The seven victims were between 51 and 59 years old.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple flash floods were reported across Utah on September 15, before the airmass dried significantly over the latter half of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred along the Paria River, with the gauge at Lees Ferry rising from near 100 cfs up to 2064 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple flash floods were reported across Utah on September 15, before the airmass dried significantly over the latter half of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused a debris flow from the Patch Springs burn scar, with debris covering State Route 199 at Johnson Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple flash floods were reported across Utah on September 15, before the airmass dried significantly over the latter half of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Park rangers at Zion National Park reported flash flooding through the area, with the worst flooding near the Emerald Pools. Waterfalls and minor rockfalls were also reported across the park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered thunderstorms formed across southwest Utah on September 13, and a severe wind gust was recorded with one storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Brimstone Reservoir RAWS recorded a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple flash floods were reported across Utah on September 15, before the airmass dried significantly over the latter half of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Additional heavy rainfall over Hildale caused minor flash flooding across the area while search and rescue operations were still ongoing from the previous day's major flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two flash floods were observed in the eastern half of Utah on July 14, including a significant flash flood in Capitol Reef National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of heavy rain caused significant flash flooding in Capitol Reef National Park. The Scenic Drive was closed due to water and debris impacting the road in multiple locations, particularly at the Grand Wash and near the Slick Rock Divide. Major flooding occurred in Capitol Gorge, and caused significant damage to the dirt road to Capitol Gorge, such that the road to this popular hiking area was closed for more than two months after the flood. Very heavy flows were also reported along Sulphur Creek and Pleasant Creek, as well as all associated washes, with water a foot deep in Sulphur Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level moisture moved into northern Utah on July 4. While some heavy rain was observed along the Wasatch Front, the strongest storms produced severe wind gusts in northwest Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts across the Great Salt Lake Desert included 71 mph at the Interstate 80 at Milepost 78 sensor and 61 mph at the Upper Cedar Mountain sensor in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture remained in place over Utah heading into the second week of July, helping produce flash flooding in both northern and southern Utah. In addition, strong thunderstorm winds were recorded in northwest Utah on July 8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large flows were reported along the East Fork of the Virgin River, with the gauge near Springdale measuring flow rates as high as 1140 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread thunderstorms occurred across Utah on July 17 and 18, with strong winds, large hail, and flash flooding all reported across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to nickel size hail was reported near Parowan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread thunderstorms occurred across Utah on July 17 and 18, with strong winds, large hail, and flash flooding all reported across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flattop Mountain RAWS recorded a maximum wind gust of 69 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread thunderstorms occurred across Utah on July 17 and 18, with strong winds, large hail, and flash flooding all reported across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 72 mph was recorded by the Joe's Valley RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture remained in place over Utah heading into the second week of July, helping produce flash flooding in both northern and southern Utah. In addition, strong thunderstorm winds were recorded in northwest Utah on July 8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind gusts from this thunderstorm included 72 mph at Vernon Hill and 66 mph at the APG sensor in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture remained in place over Utah heading into the second week of July, helping produce flash flooding in both northern and southern Utah. In addition, strong thunderstorm winds were recorded in northwest Utah on July 8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large line of thunderstorms moved through the Wasatch Front overnight on July 8, into the early morning hours of July 9, with the northern Wasatch Front getting hit the hardest by heavy rainfall. Flash flooding along roadways was reported in several locations, including along Interstate 15 and on Parrish Lane in Centerville, where water was up to four feet deep. One house in Woods Cross received significant flood damage, with all the possessions in the basement declared a total loss.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture remained in place over Utah heading into the second week of July, helping produce flash flooding in both northern and southern Utah. In addition, strong thunderstorm winds were recorded in northwest Utah on July 8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Patch Springs Fire burn scar caused a debris flow in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two flash floods were observed in the eastern half of Utah on July 14, including a significant flash flood in Capitol Reef National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Seeley Fire burn scar caused flash flooding and debris flows onto State Route 31.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread thunderstorms occurred across Utah on July 17 and 18, with strong winds, large hail, and flash flooding all reported across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused significant flooding in Hildale, with flood waters damaging both a few roads and a few homes across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread thunderstorms occurred across Utah on July 17 and 18, with strong winds, large hail, and flash flooding all reported across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 21-year-old male was riding a paddle board on the reservoir at Rockport State Park when a strong wind gust caused by a thunderstorm moved through the area. The victim was separated from his group of friends and drowned in the reservoir. His body was recovered the next day. The strongest wind gusts recorded from nearby sensors included 52 mph at the Interstate 80 at Silver Creek Junction sensor and 45 mph at the Interstate 80 at Wanship sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread thunderstorms occurred across Utah on July 17 and 18, with strong winds, large hail, and flash flooding all reported across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds impacted Interstate 80 with a 72 mph gust recorded at the Interstate 80 sensor in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet and a 64 mph wind gust recorded at the Interstate 80 at Milepost 29 sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread thunderstorms occurred across Utah on July 17 and 18, with strong winds, large hail, and flash flooding all reported across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel-size hail was reported near Meeks Cabin Reservoir, along the Utah/Wyoming border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread thunderstorms occurred across Utah on July 17 and 18, with strong winds, large hail, and flash flooding all reported across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Park rangers reported flash flooding in Zion National Park, particularly in the Pine Creek drainage. The Virgin River had significant rises from Zion National Park down to St. George, with the river gauge at St. George rising from near 30 cfs up to 2310 cfs. Flooding was also reported in several other locations across the county, including Washington City, Bloomington, and St. George, with at least a dozen different roads in these areas receiving some level of flooding throughout the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture remained in place over Utah heading into the second week of July, helping produce flash flooding in both northern and southern Utah. In addition, strong thunderstorm winds were recorded in northwest Utah on July 8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Park rangers at Zion National Park reported significant rises on the North Fork of the Virgin River, particularly through the Narrows. Hikers were temporarily stranded until the waters receded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms continued across northern Utah at times during the last week of July, and a couple of these storms produced severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Diddle Knoll sensor in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet recorded a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture remained in place over Utah heading into the second week of July, helping produce flash flooding in both northern and southern Utah. In addition, strong thunderstorm winds were recorded in northwest Utah on July 8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zion National Park rangers reported flash flooding along Clear Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture remained in place over Utah heading into the second week of July, helping produce flash flooding in both northern and southern Utah. In addition, strong thunderstorm winds were recorded in northwest Utah on July 8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum recorded wind gusts from this thunderstorm included 77 mph at the Wendover AWOS and 58 mph at the Salt Flats sensor in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture remained in place over Utah heading into the second week of July, helping produce flash flooding in both northern and southern Utah. In addition, strong thunderstorm winds were recorded in northwest Utah on July 8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported along Johnson Wash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A single severe thunderstorm was reported on June 24, producing a strong wind gust in Emery County in eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rattlesnake Bench sensor recorded a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, gusty southerly winds developed ahead of a Pacific storm system, with the strongest gusts recorded in western Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum recorded wind gusts in the Great Salt Lake Desert included 67 mph at Lakeside Mountain and 60 mph at Promontory Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, gusty southerly winds developed ahead of a Pacific storm system, with the strongest gusts recorded in western Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The US-6 at Eureka sensor recorded a peak wind gust of 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought heavy snow to primarily northern Utah starting December 21, with the heaviest snowfall in the Wasatch Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall in the northern Wasatch Front included 14 inches at both the Bountiful Bench and South Ogden, 13 inches at the Northeast Ogden Bench, and 12 inches in Bountiful and Centerville. Snow on transformers and power lines led to power outages for approximately 1,200 Rocky Mountain Power customers in Ogden and Riverdale on the 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought heavy snow to primarily northern Utah starting December 21, with the heaviest snowfall in the Wasatch Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many locations in the Wasatch Mountain Valleys received a foot or more of snow from this storm, with reports of 18 inches of snow in Liberty, 15 inches in Eden, 14 inches in Heber City, 13 inches in Park City, and 12 inches at both Wanship Dam and Morgan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought heavy snow to primarily northern Utah starting December 21, with the heaviest snowfall in the Wasatch Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mammoth-Cottonwood SNOTEL received 3.50 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 35 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought heavy snow to primarily northern Utah starting December 21, with the heaviest snowfall in the Wasatch Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Buck Flat SNOTEL received 3.70 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 40 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought gusty post-frontal winds to much of western Utah on November 2 and 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum recorded wind gusts in west central Utah included 63 mph at the U.S. 6 at Eureka sensor, and 59 mph in the Tule Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought gusty post-frontal winds to much of western Utah on November 2 and 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cedar City ASOS recorded a maximum wind gust of 60 mph, while the Milford ASOS reached a peak of 58 mph.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-moving trough that clipped northern Utah brought briefly strong winds to some locations along Utah's northern border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong downslope winds developed near Curlew Junction for a few hours, with the sensor along Utah State Route 30 near Curlew Junction recording a peak wind gust of 69 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-moving trough that clipped northern Utah brought briefly strong winds to some locations along Utah's northern border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed in the Uinta Mountains, with peak recorded wind gusts of 83 mph at the Chepeta RAWS and 77 mph at the Dry Ridge sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The majority of February was dry and warm across the Four Corners region. However,  during the last week of the month winter storms brought significant precipitation, mainly in the form of snow, to the region. This moisture pushed monthly precipitation totals to the above normal category. Despite this precipitation, long-term severe drought conditions remained in place across extreme southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions remained in place across southeast corner of Utah through the month of February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and moist winter storm brought heavy snow and strong winds to portions of northern and central Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell over the Sanpete Valley, with Fairview and Mount Pleasant both receiving 10 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and moist winter storm brought heavy snow and strong winds to portions of northern and central Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fillmore received 8 inches of snow from the storm, while Eureka reported 6 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and moist winter storm brought heavy snow and strong winds to portions of northern and central Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Salt Lake Valley received almost no snow from this event, but the Tooele Valley was hit hard on the morning of November 16, with generally 8 to 10 inches reported across Tooele City. The snow caused significant travel delays and impacts in the Tooele area with snow quickly accumulating on roadways, most notably along Utah State Route 36. In addition, the heavy snow broke many large tree branches across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and moist winter storm brought heavy snow and strong winds to portions of northern and central Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall in Utah County had a strong south to north gradient, with Levan in the southern end of the county receiving 9 inches of snow, while locations from Provo northward generally received an inch or less. Snow primarily fell during the day on November 16, with a little snow lingering into November 17.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and moist winter storm brought heavy snow and strong winds to portions of northern and central Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong post-frontal winds were observed near the Utah/Nevada border, with maximum recorded wind gusts of 70 mph at the Interstate 80 at Milepost 1 sensor, 62 mph at Interstate 80 at Milepost 29 sensor, and 62 mph at the Interstate 80 sensor in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a winter storm exited Utah, strong easterly downslope and canyon winds developed in northern Utah, with strong wind gusts recorded on November 26 and 27.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong easterly canyon and downslope winds developed along the northern Wasatch Front, with peak recorded wind gusts of 70 mph at the Portable RWIS #2 sensor, 65 mph in Farmington, and 64 mph in Centerville. Only minor damage was reported in the area, including some tree limbs knocked down, as well as minor fence and siding damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm and the associated cold front brought gusty winds to much of Utah, with the strongest winds near the Salt Lake Valley and in Castle Country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong downslope winds occurred in Castle Country after the cold front passed through the area. Maximum recorded wind gusts included 80 mph at the SR-72 at Hogan Pass sensor and 70 mph at Fremont Junction.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and strong southwest flow across the region preceded an upper trough which crossed over the area late on March 2nd through March 3rd.  This situation resulted in a prolonged and nearly continuous snowfall event mainly for the mountain areas of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This snowfall event which began in the morning of February 28th produced total snowfall amounts of 3 to 6 inches above the 5000 foot level.  Locally higher amounts included 9 inches at Blanding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought gusty post-frontal winds to much of western Utah on November 2 and 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts in the Salt Lake and Tooele valleys included 69 mph at South Mountain, 68 mph at Vernon Hill, 66 mph in Tooele, 64 mph at Stockton Bar, 62 mph at the Interstate 80 at Milepost 78 sensor, and 60 mph at both the Mouth of Parleys sensor and the Great Salt Lake Marina sensor. The high winds also knocked out power for over 5,000 customers, with multiple power lines broken by the strong winds. The winds also knocked down several branches in Tooele valley, and launched a trampoline into a neighbor's yard.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought gusty post-frontal winds to much of western Utah on November 2 and 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind gusts across Utah's northwest desert included 70 mph at the English Village and Upper Cedar Mountain sensors in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet, 64 mph at Promontory Point, and 59 mph at the Utah Test and Training Range sensor. These strong winds knocked over two semitrucks on Interstate 80, and semis were subsequently banned from that stretch of Interstate 80 for the rest of the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm and the associated cold front brought gusty winds to much of Utah, with the strongest winds near the Salt Lake Valley and in Castle Country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The strongest winds in the Salt Lake and Tooele Valleys occurred with the cold frontal passage, with peak wind gusts of 62 mph at Vernon Hill, 60 mph at Point of the Mountain, and 58 mph at Flight Park South.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An embedded low pressure system in a northwest flow aloft produced significant to heavy snowfall in portions of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 8 to 15 inches were measured across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought heavy snow to primarily northern Utah starting December 21, with the heaviest snowfall in the Wasatch Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Trial Lake SNOTEL received 2.90 inches of liquid water equivalent, or approximately 36 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, slow moving winter storm crossed Utah in the middle of December, bringing over a foot of snow to much of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of Davis and Weber Counties received over a foot of snow, with storm total snow reports of 30 inches at Bountiful Bench, 25 inches in Centerville, 20 inches in Bountiful, 17 inches in Fruit Heights, 15 inches in both South Ogden and East Layton, and 13 inches on the Northeast Ogden Bench. Over 1,000 customers were without power at some point during the event, and there were at least 50 automobile accidents caused by the snow covered roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, slow moving winter storm crossed Utah in the middle of December, bringing over a foot of snow to much of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the Salt Lake and Tooele Valleys received over a foot of snow during the event, with storm totals of 23 inches in Sandy and Cottonwood Heights, 21 inches in Upper Millcreek, 18 inches in the Upper Avenues, 17 inches in Olympus Cove, and 15 inches in Draper and Tooele. Totals were often smaller toward the northern end of the Salt Lake Valley, with the Salt Lake International Airport receiving 9.5 inches of snow; however, this included 8.2 inches on the calendar day of December 14, which broke the old record for the date of 5.8 inches in 1922. Over 150 accidents or stranded motorists were reported in the Salt Lake Valley, and at least 1,500 customers were without power at some point during the event, with many of those in Bluffdale and Draper.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, slow moving winter storm crossed Utah in the middle of December, bringing over a foot of snow to much of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall in in the northern Wasatch Mountains included 19 inches at Powder Mountain Resort and 17 inches at Snowbasin Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, slow moving winter storm crossed Utah in the middle of December, bringing over a foot of snow to much of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow amounts in the southern Wasatch Front included 27 inches in Suncrest, 12 inches in Levan, and 9 inches in Spanish Fork, Alpine, and Lehi. At least 50 automobile accidents were reported during the storm, and power outages were significant in the area, with over 7,700 people losing power in the Eagle Mountain and Cedar Fort areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, slow moving winter storm crossed Utah in the middle of December, bringing over a foot of snow to much of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall reports included 34 inches at Alta Ski Area, 31 inches at Snowbird Ski Resort, 22 inches at Brighton Resort, and 20 inches at Solitude Mountain Ski Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, slow moving winter storm crossed Utah in the middle of December, bringing over a foot of snow to much of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall in west central Utah included 11.5 inches in Fillmore and 10.7 inches in Oak City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, slow moving winter storm crossed Utah in the middle of December, bringing over a foot of snow to much of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Both Cedar City and New Harmony received about 8 inches of snow from the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, slow moving winter storm crossed Utah in the middle of December, bringing over a foot of snow to much of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pickle Keg SNOTEL received 2.30 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 24 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, slow moving winter storm crossed Utah in the middle of December, bringing over a foot of snow to much of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall in Utah's southern mountains included 20 inches at Eagle Point Ski Resort and 16.5 inches in Parowan. The Midway Valley SNOTEL received 2.40 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 24 inches of new snow. Road impacts were significant across the area; semitrailers were banned from State Route 14 between Cedar City and the Long Valley Junction with U.S. 89, and a 3-mile stretch of State Route 143 near Panguitch was completely closed down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, gusty southerly winds developed ahead of a Pacific storm system, with the strongest gusts recorded in western Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind gusts in the Salt Lake and Tooele valleys included 69 mph at the Interstate 80 at Milepost 78 sensor, 63 mph at Flight Park South, 60 mph at the Great Salt Lake Marina, and 60 mph at Stockton Bar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought heavy snow to primarily northern Utah starting December 21, with the heaviest snowfall in the Wasatch Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 3 feet of snow fell across much of the northern Wasatch Mountains, including storm totals of 43 inches at Snowbasin Resort and 37 inches at Powder Mountain.  Travel was treacherous through the mountains routes and canyons, with snow-packed roads common across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought heavy snow to primarily northern Utah starting December 21, with the heaviest snowfall in the Wasatch Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals in the Wasatch Mountains were impressive, with many locations reporting 3-4 feet of new snow. The highest total was at Brighton Resort, which received 76 inches of snow at its 9500 foot crest. Other large totals included 48 inches at Solitude Mountain Resort, 42 inches at Alta Ski Lifts, 41 inches at Park City Mountain Resort, and 38 inches at Snowbird Ski and Summer Resort. Travel was treacherous through the mountains routes and canyons, especially in Parleys Canyon and the Cottonwood Canyons, and snow-packed roads were common across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northern Utah received even more snowfall during a storm that centered on Christmas Day. In addition, Washington County in far southwest Utah also received gusty northerly winds as the storm exited the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals from the southern Wasatch Front included 14 inches in SunCrest, 9 inches in Lehi and American Fork, and 8 inches in Nephi.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northern Utah received even more snowfall during a storm that centered on Christmas Day. In addition, Washington County in far southwest Utah also received gusty northerly winds as the storm exited the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sundance Mountain Resort received the most snow from this storm with a total of 19 new inches. Other snowfall reports included 13 inches in Provo Canyon and 12 inches at both Alta Ski Lifts and Snowbird Ski and Summer Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northern Utah received even more snowfall during a storm that centered on Christmas Day. In addition, Washington County in far southwest Utah also received gusty northerly winds as the storm exited the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall reports from this relatively quick event included 10 inches in Tooele, Cottonwood Heights, and South Jordan, as well as 8.5 inches in Taylorsville, 8 inches in Kearns, and 7 inches in Sandy.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 2 to 5 inches were measured above the 5000 foot level.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall in a catch basin north of Arches National Park on the afternoon of August 30th resulted in a flash flood which reached a section of Arches National Park later that evening.  The flash flooding persisted through midday on August 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall in a catch basin north of Arches National Park on the afternoon of August 30th resulted in a flash flood which reached a section of Arches National Park later that evening and trapped many campers in the Delicate Arch Road area.  Flash flood waters were still flowing 3.5 feet deep and 50 feet wide as of noon the next day, August 31st and persisted through much of the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storms tracked across the Four Corners region and brought both rain and snowfall to that area through the month. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe long-term drought conditions remained entrenched across the far southeast corner of Utah through the month of January despite recent wetter than normal conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of Pacific storms moved across the region in May and occasionally absorbed remnants of tropical moisture which produced significant precipitation over the area. As a result, drought conditions in northeast Utah improved dramatically by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A discrete single-cell storm produced a rare and highly visible tornado in southern San Juan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 5:40 pm a rare tornado touched down near Comb Ridge east of Monument Valley in southeast Utah. The tornado drifted slowly to the northeast and roped out lasting approximately 15 minutes. No damage occurred as the tornado traveled over the open desert. This rare mesocyclonic tornado formed on the southwest corner of an uncommon long-lived, single-cell storm on the Colorado Plateau. The storm formed in far northwest Arizona and traveled over 75 miles before dissipating in western Colorado. Due to the high-visibility location of the storm near tourist attractions in Monument Valley, there were numerous eye-witness accounts and photos of this rare event. The tornado was photographed from over 25 miles away in some cases.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of an Eastern Pacific tropical weather system brought a large amount of moisture to the intermountain west during the second week of June. This moisture led to widespread precipitation, some from heavy rainfall producing thunderstorms, which in turn produced areas of flooding across portions of Eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several areas of debris filled water up to a foot deep flowed over Highway 211 between Highway 191 and the entrance to Canyonlands National Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Subtropical moisture and strong thunderstorm development resulted in heavy rainfall and some flash flooding in portions of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in a flash flood within Arch Canyon with fast moving water at least four feet deep.  An SUV that was parked along the Arch Canyon Trail road was carried down the canyon about 2.2 miles.  The people who owned the SUV were camped on higher ground and had to hike out of the canyon for assistance after the flood waters subsided.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced moderate to heavy rainfall from the Bookcliffs in eastern Utah southward into San Juan County. The rainfall allowed washes to run, which resulted in  flooding of roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county sheriff's office reported 10 to 20 campers stranded in the White Wash Sand Dune Recreation area due to impassible roadways from fast flowing water and significant mud deposited onto roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong rotating thunderstorm produced damaging outflow winds in Daggett County and continued on into Uintah County where it produced a relatively long-lived but short track tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm produced strong winds which snapped off or uprooted many coniferous and aspen trees up to two feet in diameter within the Greens Draw area. This event was experienced by some people who were in that area when it occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong rotating thunderstorm produced damaging outflow winds in Daggett County and continued on into Uintah County where it produced a relatively long-lived but short track tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The supercell thunderstorm which produced strong damaging outflow winds in Daggett County moved into nearby Uintah County and spawned a funnel cloud which was observed for well over 5 minutes before the rotation made contact with the ground.  The tornado remained over open country during its existence.  Although the rotation was on the ground for about 9 minutes, it was estimated to have only traveled about one mile during that time.  After the rotation lifted off the ground, the funnel cloud persisted for at least another 5 minutes.
